content.value: A tree is a hierarchical structure: one root node, with each node having zero or more child nodes and no cycles. A binary tree restricts each node to at most two children, conventionally called left and right.
Binary trees can also be stored in an array (common for heaps): for a node at index i, its left child is at 2i+1 and right child at 2i+2 (0-indexed), with no explicit pointers needed.

items: Preorder: visit node, then left subtree, then right subtree (Node-Left-Right)
Inorder: visit left subtree, then node, then right subtree (Left-Node-Right)
Postorder: visit left subtree, then right subtree, then node (Left-Right-Node)
Level order: visit nodes level by level, using a queue (this is BFS applied to a tree)

content.value: Inorder traversal of a Binary Search Tree always visits nodes in sorted order - this single fact is tested constantly and is worth memorizing cold.
Uses a queue: start by enqueueing the root, then repeatedly dequeue a node, print it, and enqueue its non-null children, until the queue is empty.
